coredata.dc:title: An optical and visual multi-mode sensing platform base on nitrogen, sulfur, boron co-doped carbon dots for rapid and simple determination of ferric ions in water
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Xuewen
Luo, Zhenfeng
Li, Wei
Xia, Lingfeng
Xiong, Yan
coredata.dc:description: Abnormal iron ions levels may lead to some diseases and serious environmental pollution. Herein, optical and visual detection strategies of Fe3+ in water based on co-doped carbon dots (CDs) were established in the present study. Firstly, a one-pot synthetic strategy for the preparation of the N, S, B co-doped CDs with a home microwave oven was developed. Secondly, the optical properties, chemical structures, and morphology of CDs were further characterized by fluorescence spectroscopy, Uv–vis absorption spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and transmission electron microscope. Finally, the results indicated that the fluorescence of the co-doped CDs was quenched by ferric ions via the static mechanism and the aggregation of CDs, accompanied by the increased red color. The multi-mode sensing strategies of Fe3+ with fluorescence photometer, UV–visible spectrophotometer, portable colorimeter and smartphone had the advantages of good selectivity, excellent stability and high sensitivity. Fluorophotometry based on co-doped CDs was a powerful probe platform for measuring lower concentrations of Fe3+ due to its higher sensitivity, better linear relationship, lower limit of detection (0.27 μM) and limit of quantitation (0.91 μM). In addition, the visual detection methods with a portable colorimeter and smartphone had been proven to be very suitable for rapid and simple sensing of higher concentrations of Fe3+. Moreover, the co-doped CDs utilized for Fe3+ probes in tap water and boiler water obtained satisfactory results. Consequently, the efficient, versatile optical and visual multi-mode sensing platform could be extended to apply such a visual analysis of ferric ions in the biological, chemical and other fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ferric ions
Fluorophotometry
Colorimetry
Smartphone
Visual detection

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity of the CDs for Fe3+
Stability of CDs in Fe3+ aqueous solutions

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province under Grant ( 2020JJ6098 , 2021JJ30667 ) and Scientific Research Foundation of Hunan Provincial Education Department ( 22A0107 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectroscopy of the CDs (a) and XPS spectrum of the CDs (b).
TEM, HRTEM (inset of upper) and Size distribution (inset of lower) images of CDs.

The 3D fluorescence spectrogram of the CDs (0.50 mg mL−1) aqueous solution. Fluorescence intensity increased with the color change from blue to red (a). Uv–vis absorption spectroscopy, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the CDs (0.50 mg mL−1) aqueous solution (b). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Selectivity of CDs (0.50 mg mL−1) aqueous solutions for Fe3+ over other cations at 370 nm excitation. The cyan bars represent the addition of various metal ions (1.00 mM) to CDs solution. The magenta bars represent the subsequent addition of 2.00 mM Fe3+ to CDs solution.(a) The images under daylight (upper) and 365 nm UV Lamp (lower) containing 1.0 mM of metal ions (b). The images under daylight (upper) and 365 nm UV Lamp (lower) containing 1.0 mM of diverse metal ions and 2.0 mM Fe3+(c). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

F/F0 values of CDs (0.50 mg mL−1) aqueous solutions (Fe3+, 0.06 mM) (a), the areas under 400–700 nm in the Uv–vis absorption spectroscopy (Fe3+, 0.06 mM) (b), the RGB values detected with a portable colorimeter (Fe3+, 0.20 mM) (c) and smartphone (Fe3+, 1.20 mM) (d) in 120 min.

F/F0 values of CDs (0.50 mg mL−1) aqueous solutions (Fe3+, 0.20 mM) (a), the areas under 400–700 nm in the Uv–vis absorption spectroscopy (Fe3+, 0.20 mM) (b), the RGB values detected with a portable colorimeter (Fe3+, 1.60 mM) and smartphone (Fe3+, 1.40 mM) in medium pH values.

Fluorescence quenching of CDs in the presence of different concentrations of Fe3+ (0–3600 μM) (a) and the linear relationship between (F0-F)/F of CDs and concentrations of Fe3+ (0–300 μM) at the 460 nm under the excitation wavelength of 370 nm (b).

Uv–vis absorption spectroscopy of CDs in the presence of different concentrations of Fe3+ (0–3600 μM) (a) and the linear relationship between the area under 400–700 nm and concentrations of Fe3+ at 0–3000 μM (b).

The linear relationship between the RGB values and concentrations of Fe3+ based on portable colorimeter (0–2.40 mM) (a) and smartphone (0–2.00 mM) (b). Inset: The color changes of the solution under daylight.

The fluorescence lifetime decay curves of CDs in the absence (black) and in the presence (red) of Fe3+ (a). The particle size distribution of 0.5 mg/mL CDs (solid), 0.5 mg/mL CDs + 1.0 mM Fe3+ (dash) and 0.5 mg/mL CDs + 3.0 mM Fe3+ (dot).(b) The TEM, HRTEM (inset of upper) and size distribution (inset of lower) of 0.5 mg/mL CDs + 1.0 mM Fe3+ (c) and 0.5 mg/mL CDs + 3.0 mM Fe3+ (d). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The C1s (a), N1s (b), O1s (c), S2p (d), B1s (e) and Fe2p (f) high-resolution XPS spectra of the 0.5 mg/mL CDs (upper), 0.5 mg/mL CDs + 1.0 mM Fe3+ (middle) and 0.5 mg/mL CDs + 3.0 mM Fe3+ (lower).
The possible sensing mechanism in the present study.
Detection results of Fe3+ in real samples.
Comparison of different sensors for Fe3+ detection with CDs.

head.ce:title: An optical and visual multi-mode sensing platform base on nitrogen, sulfur, boron co-doped carbon dots for rapid and simple determination of ferric ions in water

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Engineering, School of Chemical Engineering, Xiangtan University, Xiangtan 411105, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Engineering, School of Chemical Engineering, Xiangtan University, Xiangtan 411105, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Abnormal iron ions levels may lead to some diseases and serious environmental pollution. Herein, optical and visual detection strategies of Fe3+ in water based on co-doped carbon dots (CDs) were established in the present study. Firstly, a one-pot synthetic strategy for the preparation of the N, S, B co-doped CDs with a home microwave oven was developed. Secondly, the optical properties, chemical structures, and morphology of CDs were further characterized by fluorescence spectroscopy, Uv–vis absorption spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and transmission electron microscope. Finally, the results indicated that the fluorescence of the co-doped CDs was quenched by ferric ions via the static mechanism and the aggregation of CDs, accompanied by the increased red color. The multi-mode sensing strategies of Fe3+ with fluorescence photometer, UV–visible spectrophotometer, portable colorimeter and smartphone had the advantages of good selectivity, excellent stability and high sensitivity. Fluorophotometry based on co-doped CDs was a powerful probe platform for measuring lower concentrations of Fe3+ due to its higher sensitivity, better linear relationship, lower limit of detection (0.27 μM) and limit of quantitation (0.91 μM). In addition, the visual detection methods with a portable colorimeter and smartphone had been proven to be very suitable for rapid and simple sensing of higher concentrations of Fe3+. Moreover, the co-doped CDs utilized for Fe3+ probes in tap water and boiler water obtained satisfactory results. Consequently, the efficient, versatile optical and visual multi-mode sensing platform could be extended to apply such a visual analysis of ferric ions in the biological, chemical and other fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the most essential metal ions in biological and environmental systems, inadequate or excessive ferric ions (Fe3+) levels may lead to some diseases and serious environmental pollution. [1] As we all know, abnormal intake of Fe3+ will lead to cell homeostasis disorder, leading to a variety of diseases, such as anemia, heart disease, intellectual decline and cancer.[2] In addition, iron corrosion is also a subject of great technical significance and has attracted increasing public attention. It is still challenging to effectively detect iron corrosion at an earlier stage.[3] Up to now, more and more analysis methods, including spectrophotometry, colorimetry and mass spectrometry, have been established to determinate Fe3+ quantitatively and qualitatively.[4–7] However, most of these analysis approaches require expensive equipment and are time-consuming procedures, which limit their practicability.

Recently, carbon dots (CDs) as probes for Fe3+ determination have attracted considerable attention due to their simple preparation method, excellent optical properties, good biocompatibility, high chemical stability and low toxicity. For instance, an N, S co-doped CDs with high fluorescence quantum yield (QY) synthesized by microwave-assisted method realized the selective detection of Fe3+.[8] N doped-CDs in one-step solid phase synthesis from citric acid and benzoylurea also exhibited that it could serve as a sensitive “turn-off” fluorescent sensor for Fe3+ and the limit of detection (LOD) was 1.1 μM.[9] Interestingly, an Ethanol-based CDs detection of 10–600 μM Fe3+ showed excellent performance in sensitivity and selectivity.[10] A dual-property CDs synthesized via a facile onestep solvothermal method from 4-(2-pyridylazo)-resorcinol exhibited multi-functional applications in Fe3+ detection and A549 cell bioimaging.[11] A dual-function CDs based on silkworm excrement showed excellent performance in detecting Cu2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Ni2+, and Fe2+ separately under acidic conditions.[12].

More recently, CDs have been proven to be promising fluorescence and visual multi-mode sensors because of their robustness and cost-effective nature.[13–17] Growing research results show that portable colorimeters and smartphones are promising devices in portable and on-site detection technology [18–20]. A smartphone-assisted platform based on N-doped CDs encapsulated porphyrin metal–organic frameworks achieved visual quantification of glyphosate in the range of 0.01–6.67 mg/L with a low LOD of 9.06 µg/L.[21] The RGB values were obtained from the colorimetric photos, which were captured by the smartphone camera under the illumination of the built-in flashlight. In addition, the doped CDs derived from primary aromatic amine had good performance in the sensitive and accurate detection of NO2 – due to its color changes under visible and ultraviolet light.[22] The images were recorded with a smartphone camera under visible and ultraviolet lights (365 nm UV lamp). The green luminescent CDs synthesized from 4-aminophenol and ethylenediamine has also been proven to be an attractive visual detection material for selective detection of Fe3+ in the range of 0 – 0.8 mM and with a LOD of 0.05 mM. The photographs were recorded with a smartphone camera under a 365 nm UV lamp and the RBG color parameters were collected with the Color Grab application installed in the smartphone.[23] However, most visual analysis methods are based on ultraviolet irradiation. It is well known that exposure to ultraviolet irradiation has health risks. It can lead to skin sunburn and premature aging, and even related to skin cancer. Therefore, the development of rapid, economical, simple and effective visual detection methods based on CDs to sense Fe3+ with portable devices in practical applications is still needed.

Herein, we reported rapid and simple detection strategies of Fe3+ in water based on co-doped CDs. Firstly, a simple one-pot synthetic strategy to prepare the N, S, B co-doped CDs with a home microwave oven was proposed. Secondly, fluorescence spectroscopy, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and transmission electron microscope (TEM) were further applied to employ the optical properties, chemical structures and morphology of the co-doped CDs. Subsequently, optical detection strategies of Fe3+ in water with a fluorescence photometer and Uv–vis spectrophotometer were investigated. Visual detection for Fe3+ with a portable colorimeter and smartphone was also exploited. Finally, the possible sensing mechanism of the CDs was also discussed.

Lead acetate, sodium chloride, potassium chloride, calcium chloride, silver nitrate and other metal ions (Mg2+, Ni3+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Fe2+, Al3+, Hg2+, Cr3+, Cd2+, and Ba2+) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent (Shanghai, China) and Aladdin Biochemical Technology (Shanghai, China). Citric acid, thiourea and borax were purchased from Hengxing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Hydrochloric acid and Sodium hydroxide were bought from Hunan Huihong Reagent Co., Ltd (Changsha, China). The reagents used in this study were of analytical grade and then used as received. Ultrapure water was supplied from Wahaha Group Co., Ltd (Hangzhou, China).

FT-IR was observed using an AVATAR 370 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). XPS was recorded with a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). HRTEM mages were measured with a JEM-F200 high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan). Uv–vis spectra were recorded with UV8000 UV visible spectrophotometer (Shanghai metash instruments, China). The fluorescence lifetimes were measured on an Edinburgh Instruments FLS1000 equipped with the light emitting diodes (excitation wavelength 370 nm). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analyses were measured with Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern, UK). Fluorescence measurements were conducted with an F97pro fluorescence spectrometer (Shanghai Lengguan instruments, China). The RGB color parameters detection methods were recorded with a portable colorimeter (Colormeter Pro, Hangzhou China) and a smartphone (Huawei nova5, Shenzhen, China) installed with the PhotoMetrix PRO Application [24]. The weight of portable colorimeter is only about 100 g.

A one-pot synthetic strategy for the preparation of the N, S, B-doped CDs with a home microwave oven was described as follows. Typically, citric acid (2.0 g, 10.4 mmol), thiourea (2.0 g, 26.3 mmol) and borax (0.5 g, 1.3 mmol) were dissolved in 20 ml ultrapure water. After microwave (960 W, 5 min) treatment of the solution, the mixture was cooled to room temperature, the obtained dark brown solid was dissolved in 20 ml ultrapure water and treated with centrifugation (3500 rpm, 30 min). The supernatant was filtered (0.22 μm filter), and then the filtrate was dialyzed (500 Da) against ultrapure water for 72 h. The obtained purified product was kept vacuum drying at 55 °C for 48 h. Finally, the N, S, B co-doped CDs was stored at 4 °C before further characterization and use.

For Fluorophotometry and Uv–vis spectrophotometry, 1500 μL of 1.0 mg/mL CDs aqueous solution was diluted with 1400 μL of ultrapure water. Then, different concentrations of Fe3+ (100 μL) were rapidly mixed into the obtained solution. After shaking the resulting solution for 30 s at room temperature, fluorometric and Uv–vis spectra were recorded with F97pro fluorescence spectrometer, UV8000 UV visible spectrophotometer, respectively. For visual detection with a portable colorimeter, the above resulting solution was placed into a box and lighted with a 3 W white LED light strip. The LED light strip was fixed on the inside of the box to ensure uniform illumination. The portable colorimeter was locked to a fixed angle and distance from the resulting solution. Finally, RGB values were collected with the portable colorimeter. In addition, for visual detection with a smartphone application (PhotoMetrix PRO), 100 μL of 1.0 mg/mL CDs aqueous solution and 50 μL of ultrapure water were added into 96-well plates. Then, 50 μL of Fe3+ was rapidly added to the obtained solution. The remaining steps were the same as the portable colorimeter-based visual detection method. Then, RGB values were read with the smartphone application. LOD, Limit of Quantification (LOQ) were calculated by signal-to-noise ratios (S/N) of 3 and 10, respectively.[25] Typically, LOD = 3σ/k, where σ represents the standard deviation of 10 blank samples and k is the slope of the standard curve. All other experiments were repeated in triplicate.

FT-IR spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were carried out to investigate surface functional groups in the CDs (Fig. 1 ). As shown in Fig. 1a, the broad adsorption peak centered around 3430 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibrations of the NH and OH [26]. The sharp peak at 2064 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of SCN. The band around 1660 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of CO and CC [27]. The peak at 1400 cm−1 was corresponded to the stretching vibration of CN [1]. The peak around 1190 cm−1 was attributed to the bending vibration of BOH [28]. The band at 1104 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of CB [29]. The peak at 925 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of the CO bond [30].

XPS analysis was carried out to characterize the composition of on the surface of the co-doped CDs (Fig. 2 ). Fig. 2a showed three strong bands at 284.5, 399.3, 531.3, 191.5 and 164.4 eV, which were assigned to C1s, N1s, O1s, B1s and S2p respectively. In addition, Fig. 1b also revealed that the atomic percentages value of C, O, N, S and B were 59.62%, 23.64%, 10.17%, 3.17%, and 3.40%, respectively. Further analysis revealed that the C1s XPS (Fig. S1a) was fitted to four binding energy peaks corresponding to COO–(288.6 eV), CN (286.2 eV), CC/CH (284.6 eV)[31] and CB (283.1 eV) [32]. The high-resolution N1s spectrum could be deconvoluted into two peaks (Fig. S1b), situated at 400.1, 398.0 eV, respectively. The peak at 400.1 eV was ascribed to CN bond and the signals around 398.0 eV was assigned to the CN bond.[31,33] The high resolution O1S spectrum (Fig. S1c) was fitted into three characteristic peaks, which were attributed to COO– (533.5 eV), COH (532.1 eV), SO (529.8 eV), respectively.[34] As for the S2p high-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. S1d), the peaks centered around 162.2, 163.5, 164.4 and 168.5 eV revealed four forms of sulfur element [1,35]. The main peaks (162.2, and 164.4 eV) were ascribed to the CS (S2p3/2 and S2p1/2). The peak at 163.5 eV might be attributed to SCN bond. Another one peak at 168.5 eV was attributed to CSOx.[1] In addition, the B1s high-resolution spectra of B-C in Fig. S1e was a single apparent characteristic peak of BCO2, centered at 192.5 eV.[36] As a result, -SCN group has characteristic peaks in C1s, N1s, and S2p high-resolution XPS spectra, with values of 286.2, 398.0, 163.5 eV, respectively. Therefore, the results of FT-IR spectroscopy and XPS suggested that there were numerous oxygen-containing functional groups, boron-containing functional groups, sulfur-containing functional groups (SCN) and amino groups on the surface of CDs. In summary, nitrogen, sulfur and boron were successfully introduced into the co-doped CDs.

A transmission electron microscope (TEM) was used to characterize the morphology of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 2, the average size of CDs was approximately 3.7 nm and the co-doped CDs tended to aggregate in an aqueous solution. It may be related to the abundant polar groups on the surface of co-doped CDs. In addition, the TEM images also revealed that the CDs material has no lattice structure. Hence, the co-doped CDs belonged to carbon nanodots [37].

The 3D fluorescence spectra and Uv–vis absorption spectroscopy were performed to investigate the optical properties of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the 3D fluorescence spectra of the CDs solution represented a strong emission range of 400–600 nm at the broad excitation wavelength of 300–450 nm. Fig. 3a also showed that the maximum emission peak was located at 460 nm under the excitation wavelength of 370 nm. In general, with the increase of excitation wavelength, the emission peak moved to a longer wavelength, while near the maximum excitation wavelength, the emission peak changed slightly (Figure S2).

It implied that there might be discrete energy levels at the CDs [38]. The optical properties of the CDs was similar to most reported CDs, which indicated the presence of abundant organic functional groups inside or on the surface [39]. In addition, according to the method in the literature,[40] the N, S, B-doped CDs presented an unexpectedly high fluorescence QY (41%) with rhodamine B (QY = 31%) as a reference.

The Uv–vis absorption spectroscopy (Fig. 3b) exhibited that two characteristic peaks were located at 270 nm and 339 nm, which were attributed to π- π* transition of the CC bond [41]and n- π* transition of the CO bond[18], respectively. Furthermore, there was a relationship between fluorescence intensity and CDs concentration in the range of 0.025–0.75 mg mL−1(Fig. S3a). Under the concentration of 0.50 mg mL−1, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs increased dramatically with the increase in the concentration. However, when the CDs concentration was over 0.50 mg mL−1, the fluorescence intensity decreased slightly with the increasing concentration, which might be the reason for the induced quenching [42]. In addition, the CDs illustrated excellent fluorescent stability within 25 min (Fig. S3b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the CDs for Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: The F/F0 values of CDs (0.50 mg mL−1) aqueous solutions containing 1.0 mM of diverse metal ions (Mn2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Na+, Ca2+, K+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Al3+, Hg2+, Cr3+, Cd2+, and Ba2+) were measured under the excitation of 370 nm. As illustrated in Fig. 4 a and Fig. 4b, obvious fluorescence quenching occurred in the presence of Fe3+, whereas insignificant changes were inspected for other metal ions. To further determine the selectivity of CDs for Fe3+, 2.0 mM Fe3+ was added to a solution of CDs containing other ions. As shown in Fig. 4, it was very apparent that the fluorescence of the CDs almost disappears after the addition of 2.0 mM Fe3+. The results revealed that the CDs had high selectivity for Fe3+ and could be used as a good fluorescent sensor for Fe3+ detection. Simultaneously, Fig. 4b indicated that a visible color change of CDs also appeared with the addition of Fe3+, while no obvious color changes were observed in the presence of other metal ions. The excellent fluorescence and optical properties indicated that the CDs might be used as a fluorescent and visualization sensor for the detection of Fe3+ with high selectivity. Therefore, the optical and visual multi-mode sensing method, including fluorescent spectra, Uv–vis absorption spectra and RGB values visual detection methods were proposed to analyze the Fe3+ in the present study. The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra before and after adding metal ions were displayed in Figure S4 and Figure S5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of CDs in Fe3+ aqueous solutions
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of CDs was evaluated by incubation time and pH. As shown in Fig. 5 , the fluorescence intensity in fluorescent spectra, area under 400–700 nm in Uv–vis absorption spectroscopy, RGB values obtained with a portable colorimeter and smartphone were nearly no change within 120 min. There was an inconspicuous shoulder peak around 440 nm in the Uv–vis absorption spectra of carbon dots, while a slightly elevation was observed at 400–700 nm at lower concentration Fe3+. Therefore, the area under 400–700 nm in Uv–vis absorption spectroscopy was attempted to evaluate the Fe3+. Fig. 5 also demonstrated that the fluorescence intensity of CDs, the area under 400–700 nm in Uv–vis absorption spectroscopy, RGB values remained stable upon adding Fe3+ for 1 min. Meanwhile, the pH stability of CDs was investigated. Fig. 6 revealed that the values of CDs in the presence of Fe3+ obtained with the above four methods were stable under moderate pH conditions. All these results indicated that the CDs possessed excellent stability under various environmental conditions, which provided the possibility of rapid and simple detection of Fe3+ in solutions.

Quantitative determination of Fe3+ ions in aqueous solutions were established using the above four methods. As shown in Fig. 7 , the fluorescence quenching at 460 nm was observed with increasing Fe3+ concentration, showing a complex exponential relationship. However, the linear relationship within 0–300 μM followed the Stern Volmer equation, F0/F = 1 + KsvC = 1 + Kqτ0C. Here, Ksv represents the slope of linear fitting, Kq represents the quenching constant, τ0 represents the fluorescence lifetime, C represents the Fe3+ concentration, and F0, F represent the fluorescence intensity before and after adding Fe3+, respectively. As a result, linear regression equation was (F0-F)/F = 0.00312*C + 0.0155 (R2 = 0.9965) at a range of 0–300 μM. The LOD and LOQ were calculated to be 0.27, 0.91 μM, respectively. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the slightly elevation was observed at 400–700 nm in the Uv–vis absorption spectra after adding Fe3+, which was attributed to the binding of Fe3+ with the functional groups on the surface of CDs. Hence, the area under 400–700 nm in Uv–vis absorption spectroscopy was used to evaluate the Fe3+ in aqueous solutions. Fig. 8b showed that the Uv–vis absorption spectra curve at 400–700 nm increased slightly with the addition of Fe3+. The linear regression equation was Area = 48.22 + 0.074*C (R2 = 0.9966) at a broad range of 0–3600 μM. The LOD and LOQ were 1.87 μM and 6.20 μM, respectively.

The color of the CDs solution gradually varied from yellowish to brownish red under daylight with the increase of Fe3+ concentration. Therefore, visual detection methods based on RGB values were also developed to determine the Fe3+. Fig. 9 revealed the RGB values decreased with the increase of Fe3+. For the portable colorimeter, the linear regression equations were ValueR = 70.36–8.47*C (R2 = 0.9963), ValueG = 60.59–10.04*C (R2 = 0.9462), ValueB = 47.77–2.45*C (R2 = 0.9604) at 0–2.40 mM (Fig. 9a). It was obvious that the linear relationship of R values vs Fe3+ concentration was better. Then, the LOD and LOQ were 1.33 μM and 4.43 μM calculated with linear regression equations of R values. Similarly, for the smartphone application, the linear regression equations were ValueR = 76.22–3.40*C (R2 = 0.9875), ValueG = 12.38–46.03*C (R2 = 0.9759), ValueB = 175.27–86.94*C (R2 = 0.9785) at 0–2.00 mM (Fig. 9b), respectively. Obviously, the slope of the ValueB linear regression equation was the highest. Therefore, the LOD and LOQ were calculated to be 1.09 and 3.29 μM, respectively.

The above calibration curves had a good linear relationship, indicating that the prepared doped CDs might be an excellent optical and visual detection sensor material for rapid and simple discrimination of Fe3+ ions. Importantly, the LODs of the above four methods were lower than the maximum level of Fe3+ (5.4 μM) in drinking water allowed by World Health Organization (WHO) and China’s drinking water standard (GB5749-2022). In addition, the fluorimetric analysis method showed more sensitivity, a better linear relationship, lower LOD and LOQ, which indicated that it was suitable for the measurement of lower concentration Fe3+. The analysis method based on Uv–vis absorption spectra could be used for monitoring and measuring the concentration of Fe3+ within a wide concentration range (0–3600 μM), but the data processing was complex. Meanwhile, the visual detection methods with a portable colorimeter and smartphone enabled rapid and simple determination of higher concentrations of Fe3+.

The water samples were collected from tap water and boiler water in a local school. To evaluate the feasibility of the prepared co-doped CDs for discrimination of Fe3+ in real samples, the water samples were then spiked with the known concentration of Fe3+. As could be seen in Table 1 , the recoveries of added Fe3+ (2.00, 20.00, 200.00 μM) in the real sample based on fluorophotometry ranged from 90.05% to 106.50%, and relative standard deviations (RSD) were less than 4.69%. For Uv–vis spectrophotometry, the recoveries of added Fe3+ ranged from 90.50% to 114.50%, and the RSDs ranged from 0.48% to 4.76%, respectively. The recoveries detected with the portable colorimeter ranged from 88.00% to 115.50%, and the RSDs were less than 7.61%. For the other visual detection method with smartphone PhotoMetrix Pro APP, the recoveries of Fe3+ were from 84.50% to 110.50%, and the RSDs ranged from 0.31% to 9.21%, respectively.

Furthermore, the fluorescent methods based on CDs for the determination of Fe3+ reported in nearly three years were listed in Table 2 . Compared with other carbon dots, the developed versatile multi-mode sensor platform in the present study has outstanding performance in good sensitivity, high selectivity, and rapid response.

Fig. 7b demonstrated that the concentration of Fe3+ and (F0-F)/F did not followed the Stern Volmer equation within the range of 0–3000 μM, which was a complex exponential relationship, indicating the complex fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs. Therefore, the possible sensing mechanism of the multi-mode sensor was further investigated by using fluorescence lifetime, size parameters, TEM and XPS of the co-doped CDs. The average fluorescence lifetimes of the co-doped CDs (Fig. 10 a) in the absence, in the presence of Fe3+ were 9.46 ns, 7.83 ns, respectively. The shorter fluorescence lifetime in the presence of Fe3+ might be caused by the binding of Fe3+ with the functional groups on the surface of CDs. The linear relationship within 900–3000 μM also followed the Stern Volmer equation (Figure S6). The linear regression equation was (F0-F)/F = 0.027C-24.24 (R2 = 0.9563). Therefore, the quenching constant (Kq = Ksv/τ0) was 3.3 × 1011 M−1s−1 at lower Fe3+ concentrations (0–300 μM) and 2.7 × 1012 M−1s−1 at higher Fe3+ concentrations (900–3000 μM), respectively. Both were much greater than the maximum value of dynamic quenching (2 × 1010 M−1s−1), indicating the involvement of static mechanism and the formation of non-luminescent complexes.

On the other hand, DLS analysis was used for measuring the average particle size of the CDs aqueous solution in the absence, in the presence of Fe3+. As shown in Fig. 10b, the size of the co-doped CDs (0.5 mg/mL) was 278 nm, which was significantly smaller than that of CDs after the addition of Fe3+. Moreover, the particle size increased with the increase of Fe3+ concentration. The TEM images (Fig. 2, Fig. 10c and Fig. 10d) also revealed the aggregation of the CDs after the addition of Fe3+ ion. When the concentration of Fe3+ increased from 1 mM to 3 mM, the average sizes of CDs increased from 3.7 nm to about 30 nm. The difference in average size measured using DLS and TEM was due to the fact that the DLS method measures the hydrodynamic radius of particles.[53] In addition, the HRTEM images further indicated that the prepared co-doped CDs exhibited highly crystalline structures. Upon addition of 1 mM, 3 mM Fe3+ to aqueous solution of CDs, the lattice stripe spacing was 0.27 nm and 0.31 nm, respectively, indicating that the binding of Fe3+ with the surface functional groups of CDs changed the size and structures of CDs.

To further explored the binding of Fe3+ with the surface functional groups of CDs, the XPS (Figure S7) were recorded before and after adding Fe3+ to the CDs aqueous solution. The relative contents of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and boron elements on the surface of CDs varied significantly (Fig. 12a-f). As shown Fig. 12a, the C-B group (C1s, 283.1 eV) was disappeared, while the carboxyl group (C1s, 288.6 eV) and the S-C≡N group (C1s, 286.2 eV) were obviously weakened with addition of Fe3+ to the CDs. It was observed that the SCN group (N1s, 398.0 eV) was also significantly weakened in the N1s high-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. 12b), which was consistent with the results of C1s high-resolution XPS spectra. In addition, a new peak (N1s, 402.0 eV) was appeared, which was attributed to N-Fe bond.[31] The signals of C-SOx in O1s (Fig. 12c) and S2p (Fig. 12d) high-resolution XPS spectra significantly increased with the increase of Fe3+. The S2p high-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. 12d) also demonstrated the signal enhancement of C-S group (S2p, 164.4 eV) and the signal attenuation of S-C≡N group with addition of Fe3+. Fig. 12e indicated that the BCOO group was obviously vanished in the B1s XPS spectra of CDs with addition of Fe3+. The Fe2p high-resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. 12f) could be deconvoluted into four main peaks, the peaks around 711.1 and 724.9 eV were assigned to the Fe3+ 2p3/2 and Fe3+ 2p1/2, respectively[54], and the peaks around 714.8 and 729.7 eV was attributed to their satellites.[55] Thus, it can be inferred that the fluorescence quenching mechanism involved the binding of Fe3+ with the surface functional groups (COOH, C-NH2, B-COOH, SCN and C-SOx) of CDs to form ground state complexes, promoting the aggregation and quenching of CDs. In addition, in accordance with Fig. 7, Fig. 8 and the color changes of the CDs solution, it was speculated that the fluorescence quenching of CDs by Fe3+ should be two stages (Fig. 11
).

FT-IR and XPS (Fig. 1) revealed that there were abundant functional groups on the surface of the co-doped CD, such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, SCN, SOx and amino groups. DLS experiment and TEM images also showed that the CDs tended to aggregate in aqueous solution. In general, Fe3+ has a strong affinity to the co-doped CDs containing carboxyl, hydroxyl, SCN and amino groups. In the first stage (low concentration Fe3+), the fluorescence quenching was realized via static mechanism. The chelation of Fe3+ and CDs mainly occurred intra-CDs in this stage. Subsequently, after the introduction of higher concentration Fe3+, the chelation occurred inter-CDs and the particle size of CDs increased. Interestingly, with the addition of Fe3+, the particle size increasing, color changing, and fluorescence quenching occurred simultaneously. The color changed due to the red complex formed by the reaction of Fe3+ and SCN on the surface of co-doped CDs. Therefore, the optical and visual multi-mode sensor platform were used to visual detection methods Fe3+ in real water samples with outstanding performances. Compared with the traditional detection methods, the multi-mode sensor platform, especially visual detection methods, are simple, fast, efficient and non-expensive method for Fe3+ measurement, and can be completed within 3 min. More importantly, the visual detection platform could be used for portable detection, because the weight of portable colorimeter was only about 100 g.

In summary, rapid and simple identification strategies of Fe3+ in water based on co-doped CDs were described in the study. The prepared co-doped CDs with strong fluorescence have realized multi-mode sensing for iron ions due to its good selectivity, excellent stability and high sensitivity. The results demonstrated that the optical and visual multi-mode sensing approaches using fluorescence photometer, portable colorimeter, and smartphone were suitable for rapid and simple determination of Fe3+. In addition, the efficient and versatile optical and visual detection platform could be extended to apply such a visual analysis in the biological, chemical and environmental fields.

Xuewen Wu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft. Zhenfeng Luo: Methodology, Data curation, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Wei Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Visualization, Resources. Lingfeng Xia: Methodology, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Yan Xiong: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province under Grant (2020JJ6098, 2021JJ30667) and Scientific Research Foundation of Hunan Provincial Education Department (22A0107).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.122995.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
